10: veshafachti al beit David veal yoshev Yerushalayim ruach chen vetachanunin vehibitu elai et asher daqaru vesafdu alav kemisped al hayachid vehamer alav kemispod al habechor: 'And I will pour out on the house of David and the inhabitants of Jerusalem a spirit of grace and pleas for mercy, so that, when they look on me, on him whom they have pierced, they shall mourn for him, as one mourns for an only child, and weep bitterly over him, as one weeps over a firstborn.' The grammatical anomaly is striking: 'they shall look on me [YHWH speaking] whom they have pierced' — the divine speaker identifies himself as the one pierced. The transition from 'me' to 'him' within the verse is unexplained in the OT but is resolved in the NT: YHWH and the one who was pierced are identified — the one pierced at the crucifixion is YHWH in the person of the Son.
1: massa devar YHWH al Yisrael ne’um YHWH noteh shamayim veyosed aretz veyotzer ruach adam beqirbo: ‘An oracle — the word of the LORD concerning Israel. This is the declaration of the LORD, who stretched out the heavens, laid the earth’s foundations, and formed the human spirit within a person.’ The introduction to the second major oracle-collection (Zechariah 12-14) establishes YHWH’s authority through three creation acts: noteh shamayim (stretching out the heavens — cosmic), yosed aretz (founding/establishing the earth — geological), and yotzer ruach adam beqirbo (forming the human spirit within — anthropological). The three-level creation (heavens, earth, inner person) frames what follows as a declaration from the Creator who commands all three domains. The formation of the ruach adam (human spirit) by YHWH is the most intimate of the three — making the forthcoming divine action (pouring out the spirit of grace, Zechariah 12:10) both consistent with and the fulfillment of the Creator’s ongoing formation of the human inner life.
2: hineni sam et Yerushalayim saf ra’al lechol ha’ammim saviv vegam al Yehudah yihyeh bamatzor al Yerushalayim: ‘I am going to make Jerusalem a cup that sends all the surrounding peoples reeling.’ Saf ra’al (cup of reeling/staggering/trembling) — the cup of divine judgment that makes its drinkers stagger is the standard OT metaphor for overwhelming divine wrath poured out (Isaiah 51:17-23: the cup of staggering; Jeremiah 25:15-17: the cup of the wine of wrath). Jerusalem herself becomes the cup — those who attack her find themselves intoxicated and incapacitated by the attempt. The dynamic is the active divine protection of Jerusalem through her very helplessness: what appears vulnerable becomes the instrument of the attackers’ undoing.
3: vehayah vayom hahu asim et Yerushalayim even ma’amasah lechol ha’ammim: ‘On that day I will make Jerusalem a heavy stone for all the nations. Everyone who tries to lift it will injure themselves severely.’ Even ma’amasah (a burdensome/heavy stone for lifting) — the stone that is too heavy to lift, injuring those who strain to move it. The image shifts from the passive cup to the active burden: those who attempt to carry off or control Jerusalem find themselves injured by the very object they seek to possess. The stone image connects with Zechariah 3:9 (the stone with seven eyes) and Zechariah 10:4 (the cornerstone from Judah) — the stone theology running through Zechariah points ultimately to Christ as the cornerstone who becomes a stone of stumbling for those who reject him (1 Peter 2:6-8).
4: bayom hahu ne’um YHWH akeh chol sus batimahon verochhevo bishigaon: ‘On that day, declares the LORD, I will strike every horse with panic and every rider with madness.’ The striking of horses and riders with panic (timahon) and madness (shigaon) is YHWH’s use of psychological disorientation as a military weapon — the same divine strategy used at the Exodus (Exodus 14:24: YHWH looked down on the Egyptian army and threw it into panic), at Jericho (Josh 6), and at Gideon’s battle (Judges 7:22). YHWH does not need superior arms; he disables the enemy’s arms through panic and confusion. The watchers over Jerusalem — et beit Yehudah epqach (I will open my eyes over the house of Judah) — are directly protected while their attackers are disabled.
5: ve’amru alufei Yehudah belibbam ametz li yoshevei Yerushalayim baYHWH tzvaot Eloheihem: ‘Then the clans of Judah will say in their hearts, “The inhabitants of Jerusalem draw their strength from the LORD of Hosts, their God.”’ The internal recognition by Judah’s clans — saying “in their hearts” rather than aloud — is the quiet dawning of understanding that Jerusalem’s strength comes from YHWH, not from its own resources. The phrase ametz li yoshevei Yerushalayim baYHWH tzvaot (strength for me are the inhabitants of Jerusalem in/through YHWH of Hosts) is an affirmation of the source of covenantal security: not military alliance, not fortifications, but YHWH’s direct protection of his city and people.
6: bayom hahu asim et alufei Yehudah kecior esh be’etzim uchapad esh bamer uachalufei saviv miyamin umissemol et kol ha’ammim saviv: ‘On that day I will make the clans of Judah like a fire pot among logs, like a flaming torch among sheaves. They will consume all the surrounding peoples on the right and on the left.’ The fire imagery — cior esh be’etzim (fire pot among logs) and chapad esh bamer (flaming torch among sheaves) — is the OT image of irresistible and consuming divine-empowered warfare. The fuel that the surrounding nations provide for the fire of Judah is the reversal of their intended conquest: the attackers become the fuel for the defenders. The simultaneous right-and-left consuming ensures no escape route is left for the enemies of YHWH’s people.
7: vehoshi’a YHWH et ohalei Yehudah barishonah: ‘The LORD will save the settlements of Judah first, so that the prestige of the house of David and of Jerusalem’s inhabitants does not become greater than that of Judah.’ The divine decision to save the rural settlements (ohalei Yehudah, tents/settlements of Judah) before the city is an egalitarian reversal of the usual priority order (cities before rural areas). YHWH deliberately prevents the elevation of the Jerusalem establishment over the rural communities — a theme consistent with the Prophets’ critique of urban centralization and the OT’s concern for the periphery. The glory of the house of David and the city should not be magnified at the expense of the broader people.
8: bayom hahu yagen YHWH be’ad yoshev Yerushalayim vehayah hanichshal bahem bayom hahu keDavid: ‘On that day the LORD will protect the inhabitants of Jerusalem; even the weakest among them will be like David on that day.’ The eschatological elevation of the weakest (hanichshal, the stumbler/weakest) to Davidic strength — and the house of David to divine/angelic status — is the escalating pattern of eschatological empowerment. The weakest inhabitant of Jerusalem becomes like David (the paradigmatic warrior-king); the house of David becomes like God/the Angel of the LORD (ke-Elohim keMelach YHWH). The elevation of the weakest is the OT form of the NT beatitude: “the last will be first, and the first will be last” (Matthew 20:16).
9: vehayah bayom hahu avaqesh lehashid et kol hagoyim haba’im al Yerushalayim: ‘On that day I will set out to destroy all the nations that attack Jerusalem.’ The divine declaration of purpose against the attacking nations — avaqesh (I will set out/seek) to destroy them — frames the entire eschatological battle as YHWH’s active initiative, not a defensive reaction. The phrase is the transition from the protection of Jerusalem (Zechariah 12:1-8) to the outpouring of the spirit (Zechariah 12:10): YHWH’s decisive military action against the nations is the context within which the spiritual transformation of the house of David and Jerusalem (Zechariah 12:10) takes place. The outpouring of grace is not in spite of the judgment but alongside it.
11: bayom hahu yigdal hamispod biYerushalayim kemispod Hadadrimmon beviqqat Megiddon: ‘On that day the mourning in Jerusalem will be as intense as the mourning for Hadad-rimmon in the plain of Megiddo.’ Hadadrimmon — either a place name in the valley of Jezreel or a reference to the mourning for King Josiah, who was killed at Megiddo (2 Kings 23:29-30; 2 Chronicles 35:24-25). The mourning for Josiah — the most beloved of Judah’s kings, who died unexpectedly in battle — was the paradigmatic great mourning of OT history. Using it as the comparison for the mourning over the pierced one of Zechariah 12:10 signals the intensity and the grief-character of the mourning: this is not merely ritual mourning but the profound grief of a people who have recognized what they have done and lost.
12: vesafedah ha’aretz mishpachot mishpachot levad: ‘The land will mourn, each family privately.’ The careful enumeration of mourning family by family — David, Nathan, Levi, Shimei, and all remaining families, each in private with men and women separated — is an elaboration that emphasizes the personal, individual, non-communal character of the mourning. The separation of men and women in mourning (Zechariah 12:12-14) is unusual and may reflect the practices of deepest mourning (as in Joel 2:16: separate the bridegroom and bride). The individual, private mourning of each family is the NT form of personal conviction before God: “they were cut to the heart and said to Peter and the rest of the apostles, Brothers, what shall we do?” (Acts 2:37).
13: mishpachat beit Levi levad vesheihem levad mishpachat Shimi levad vensheihem levad: ‘The family of the house of Levi in private, with the women separately; the family of Shimei in private, with the women separately.’ The inclusion of the priestly tribe (Levi) in the mourning — even those whose official function would be to lead worship rather than mourn in private — underscores the universality and depth of the mourning. Shimei (a sub-branch of Levi, Numbers 3:17-21) is specifically named alongside Levi, and then “all remaining families” in Zechariah 12:14, ensuring that no clan or class escapes the mourning. The priestly families mourn like everyone else before the one they have pierced — there is no priestly exemption from personal grief over the rejection of the shepherd-king.
14: kol hamishpachot hanish’arot mishpachot mishpachot levad vensheihem levad: ‘And all the remaining families, each in private, with the women separately.’ The closing verse of the mourning section confirms its universality by the phrase kol hamishpachot (all the families) — a totality encompassing every family in the land. The repeated formula “in private, with the women separately” functions as a liturgical refrain, its repetition emphasizing the thoroughness of the mourning. The universality of grief — every family, every individual, men and women alike — is the OT picture of the final mourning that Revelation 1:7 describes: “every eye will see him, even those who pierced him, and all tribes of the earth will wail on account of him.”
